artifacts.content:
Mami,

I’m finally home and the house is quiet for a second, so I wanted to write before I forget half of what’s happened this month.

First, yes, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village is still my whole life. The site is that same 185.4‑acre chunk out by West Harper Road and County Road 27, just past where Ana and I met the mobile crisis counselor in August 2022 when she had that panic attack driving back to Sacramento. Every time I’m out there I picture us standing in that gravel turnout waiting for the clinician and it makes all the traffic‑safety talk feel very personal.

Work has gotten even more political. At the August 2023 Planning Commission study session, residents were furious about how little tree canopy there is around Harper Elementary and all the asthma. Afterward, Commissioner Linda Park pulled me into her office at City Hall and basically said she was afraid that if we highlighted the higher asthma rates and lack of shade in the environmental justice section, it could hurt her November 2024 reelection because two big industrial owners in that census tract are her donors. I just nodded, but I came home buzzing with anxiety and barely slept.

On top of that, we’re still dealing with Papá’s health. Ever since his minor stroke in March 2024 at Woodland Memorial, I’ve been driving from Riverbend to Woodland almost every evening for weeks at a time so he isn’t alone with Mom while she figures out his meds. I’m trying to review greenhouse gas and heat‑island memos in hospital waiting rooms. It’s not sustainable, but I don’t know what else to do.

Anyway, we’re coming Sunday with pan dulce. I’ll call you tomorrow about groceries.

Te quiero,
Laura

Ana,

I needed to vent to someone who isn’t on the City payroll.

You remember how the project area is mostly AG‑20 and UR zoning with Urban Reserve and Agriculture in the General Plan, plus that skinny Low Density Residential strip along the east side? On October 3, 2023 Karen had me write a staff memo mapping all of that along the future collector corridor. Then she asked me—very “casually”—to highlight which parcels might be easiest to assemble if Golden Valley Holdings (yes, her husband’s company) ever wanted to buy in. I did the map, but it has been sitting like a rock in my stomach ever since.

It’s not the first time either. Back on December 12, 2024 we were in closed session with City Attorney Michael Hsu about the Alvarez family’s AG‑20 parcels at 1023 and 1065 Riverbend Road. Council was floating the idea of downzoning part of their Urban Reserve acreage to Open Space. Michael and I had to walk them through how that could affect the Alvarezes’ property tax appeal and the informal CEQA settlement talks from their March 2023 lawsuit. I sat there thinking, “If this were Papá’s land, I’d be furious.”

All of this on top of the DA side letter we signed September 3, 2023 to let Sierra Vista Partners phase their $1.2M community benefits payment because Carlos Medina admitted they’re under cash‑flow pressure from that failed subdivision in Woodland where Papá used to service their equipment back in 2019. My work brain and family brain keep colliding.

Anyway, tell me about your patients, something normal. I miss you.

Besos,
Lau

Hi Ms. Nguyen,

Thank you again for meeting with us about Sofia’s schedule. I wanted to give you a little more context from the mom side.

Feather River Community Charter is so close to my world right now. The Riverbend Mixed‑Use Village site is basically in the same unincorporated urban fringe where your campus sits, and it’s within 2 miles of Riverbend High and Harper Elementary too. With Daniel teaching chemistry at Riverbend High, Mateo in 4th at Harper, and Sofia in 8th with you, it sometimes feels like every decision I make at work lands right on top of our family.

You already know about Sofia’s panic attack during the lockdown drill in March 2024. What I probably didn’t say clearly is how much that changed the way I schedule public meetings. I’ve been deliberately steering the more contentious workshops away from the school so she doesn’t have to walk past protest signs or TV cameras on her way to class.

We also had that rough stretch last fall when her attendance dropped below 70% because she was so anxious about walking past the construction staging area on Riverside Drive. Around the same time, we pulled her out for a bit after the panic attack and started weekly therapy with Dr. Alvarez at Riverbend Behavioral Health Clinic. She re‑enrolled on a reduced schedule in January 2024, and the IEP for anxiety and ADHD you helped put in place in August 2023 has made a real difference.

I know you have to keep a professional distance, but it helps me to be honest about how intertwined my job and her school life are. Thank you for seeing her as more than just another anxious kid.

Best,
Laura Castillo

metadata.subject: Sofia’s transition back and project meetings near campus

artifacts.content:
Personal planning / reflection – Riverbend Mixed‑Use Village

I keep trying to separate “planner brain” from “daughter/mom brain,” but the project description is basically a collage of our last few years.

Mom’s minor stroke in March 2022 at Woodland Memorial is why I pushed so hard for the small medical clinic in the village core and the senior‑friendly units near transit and the community center. I keep picturing her trying to get to basic care without a 20–30 minute drive.

Mateo’s severe asthma attack at Harper Elementary in October 2021, and the late‑night run to Mercy General in Sacramento, are in the back of my mind every time I write about the 3.5‑acre joint‑use school site and “safe routes to school.” I will never put his story in a public document, but that’s what I’m thinking about when I talk about cutting idling and traffic emissions around schools.

Sofia’s counselor, Ms. Alvarez, telling me in Spring 2023 that some of her 8th‑graders’ families are doubled or tripled up in west Riverbend apartments is why I keep defending the full 725‑unit residential program—especially the townhomes and multifamily. Overcrowding is not abstract to me.

And then there’s the energy piece. During the 2020 PSPS events, when we were juggling Ana’s night shifts at UC Davis and a $1,200 overdue PG&E bill, the idea of a 1.5‑acre community solar garden and more predictable local energy stopped being a “nice sustainability feature” and became a survival thing in my head.

Note to self: keep the official language technical, but don’t forget these are the real reasons I care.

metadata.title: Why the project description looks the way it does

artifacts.content:
Hi Carla,

I’m writing from my personal account because this is more about my sanity than a formal union issue (at least for now).

You probably heard that Mark Henderson is our lead Public Works traffic engineer on the Riverbend Mixed‑Use Village. At an internal coordination meeting at City Hall in October 2023, he pulled me aside and told me about his DUI arrest in Chico the previous month. He’s worried project opponents will use it at the Planning Commission hearing to discredit the traffic impact analysis. I don’t know how to support him as a colleague without jeopardizing the project.

Layered on top of that, there’s the airport politics. The site is within about 2 miles of Riverbend Municipal Airport. Back in November 2021 I actually filed an internal complaint with HR because my then‑supervisor, Mark Ellison, was pressuring me to “soften” our description of the airport safety constraints in the draft Specific Plan to keep a developer happy. I cited the proximity to the airport and the noise/approach constraints; it felt like a line I couldn’t cross.

On the personal side, Daniel had his own scary moment at that airport in March 2023—clipped a runway light on a night landing in the Cessna and had to do a mandatory FAA safety course. Between that and a $425 noise‑violation citation we had to contest in August 2020 after a neighbor complained about his late‑night engine run‑ups, I’m hyper‑aware of how close the airport is to the Village site.

I’m exhausted from trying to be the “neutral” project manager while all these very human messes swirl around the same 185 acres. I’d appreciate any advice on boundaries.

Thanks,
Laura

metadata.subject: Airport, traffic, and trying to stay neutral

artifacts.content:
Event: Sunday visit with Mom and Papá – Woodland

Date: Sunday, March 24, 2024
Time: 10:30 a.m. – 6:30 p.m.
Location: Elena & Jorge’s house, Woodland; Woodland Memorial Hospital (if needed)
Attendees: Laura, Daniel, Sofia, Mateo, Elena, Jorge

Notes:
– Leave 814 Riverbend Landing by 10:30 so we’re at the house before lunch.
– Bring pan dulce from the bakery on Riverside Drive and the low‑sodium caldo recipe Dr. Patel at Woodland Memorial gave Mom after her minor stroke earlier this month.
– Go over Papá’s new medication schedule with him again; he keeps mixing up the evening blood pressure pill and the cholesterol one.
– Talk gently with Mom about whether she wants to keep driving at night after everything that happened on SR‑32 near Riverbend Ave in November 2022 when Papá had that little fender‑bender despite the cardiologist warning him about dizzy spells.
– If Mom is up for it, walk with her around the block and see how far she can go without knee pain.
– On the drive back, check in with Daniel about his anxiety driving past the SR‑99 / SR‑32 interchange where his former student was killed in the May 2021 DUI crash; offer to switch drivers before that segment if he seems tense.
– Reminder to self: don’t open the laptop after we get home. Just baths, homework triage, and early bed.

Daniel,

I’m still wired from tonight’s Council meeting and needed to debrief with you a bit.

They finally adopted the special Development Agreement condition for the left‑turn pocket and pedestrian refuge island at the Riverbend High/Harper access on Sierra Avenue—tied directly to the February 2, 2023 crash that injured your 10th‑grader. When staff put the CHP report and collision data up on the screen, I could feel the room shift. I kept thinking about that email you sent me from your school account with the student’s name and injuries and how we had to scrub all of that out for the public record.

It reminded me of the safe‑routes DA provision from May 7, 2024 too—the $275,000 package Sierra Vista Partners has to fund around Feather River Charter and Harper after the April 10 PTA safety meeting. Hearing you talk at that meeting about Sofia’s and Mateo’s near‑miss at the Oak Street crosswalk was one of the few times I’ve seen Council really listen to a parent, not just a planner.

And then there’s the emergency DA amendment from February 13, 2024 for the 18 low‑income families at Riverside Mobile Home Park. Mark Delaney’s photos and Maria Ortega’s public health notes about the mold and structural failures at 1020 Riverside Drive were awful. Knowing those families will get temporary units in Phase 1 of the Village helps, but it also makes me furious we let things get that bad.

All of it together—the injured student, our kids’ near‑miss, the Riverside families—makes the project feel heavier than any map or traffic model can show.

I’m going to try to sleep now. Wake me if your brain won’t shut off either.

Love,
L

metadata.subject: After the Sierra Ave / DA votes tonight
